structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records from a single hospital system covering 24 months of knee replacement surgeries. All pre-operative MRI scans (DICOM format, standardized acquisition protocols), post-operative recovery time (defined as days from surgery to first full weight-bearing clearance or discharge, whichever occurs later), demographic data (age, sex, BMI), clinical history (comorbidities: diabetes, hypertension, osteoarthritis severity grade), surgical details (surgeon ID, surgical approach, implant type, operative time), and post-operative care protocol version (enumerated by date range). Target: 400–600 patients with complete records across both modalities.
structured_choices.label_definition: Recovery time is the interval in days from surgery date to the first clinical event meeting full functional recovery: either (1) first documented full weight-bearing clearance by physical therapist assessment, or (2) hospital discharge if weight-bearing clearance occurred post-discharge. Both dates are extracted from structured clinical notes and verified against surgical logs. Any ambiguity is resolved by a blinded orthopedic clinician; patients with missing or conflicting records are excluded.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified temporal split with site/surgeon balancing. The 24-month dataset is divided chronologically: months 1–14 for training (n ≈ 280 patients), months 15–18 for validation (n ≈ 90 patients), months 19–24 for test (n ≈ 90 patients). Stratification occurs within each temporal window by (1) post-operative care protocol version (to isolate care effects from model effects) and (2) tertiles of BMI and age (to ensure demographic balance). This temporal split prevents future information leakage and mirrors deployment conditions (the model predicts on prospective patients). The care protocol stratification ensures that both train and test sets reflect the same distribution of care protocols; confound control is addressed via separate analysis (see below).
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–14) and applied identically to validation and test sets. For demographic/clinical features: (1) numeric scaling (standardization: z-score normalization) is fit on training set statistics; (2) categorical encoding (one-hot for surgeon ID, surgical approach; ordinal for osteoarthritis grade) uses training set vocabulary; (3) missing values in clinical history are imputed via median/mode (training set) and applied to val/test. For MRI images: (1) pixel-level standardization is computed from the training set image distribution (mean and std per channel); (2) any image resampling to uniform resolution (if needed) uses training set statistics. This prevents data leakage and ensures validation/test reflect realistic deployment conditions.
structured_choices.features: Baseline regression model features: (a) demographics (age, sex, BMI as continuous); (b) clinical history (comorbidity count, osteoarthritis severity grade, prior orthopedic procedures); (c) surgical factors (operative time, implant type, surgical approach). CNN model: raw MRI scans (pre-processed to 256×256×3 or equivalent standardized resolution, grayscale or RGB as acquired) plus the same demographic/clinical/surgical features concatenated to CNN feature maps before final regression head. Crucially, the CNN and regression model receive the same non-imaging feature set to ensure fair comparison; the hypothesis tests whether imaging adds predictive value beyond these features, not whether more features improve performance.
structured_choices.model: CNN architecture: ResNet-18 (pretrained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on training set MRI images) or equivalent 2D CNN backbone, with output feature maps flattened and concatenated with demographic/clinical/surgical features (7–10 numeric/categorical features). Final regression head: 2–3 fully connected layers (512 → 128 → 1 neuron) with ReLU activation. Total parameters ≈ 12M. Loss: mean squared error (MSE). Optimizer: Adam with learning rate 1e-4, early stopping on validation MSE (patience 10 epochs). Batch size: 32. No dropout or L2 regularization initially; model selection determines if regularization improves validation performance.
structured_choices.baseline: Gradient boosting regression model (XGBoost) trained on the same demographic, clinical, and surgical features (excluding MRI images). Feature engineering: identical to CNN setup (standardization, encoding, imputation, all fit on training set). XGBoost hyperparameters tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set using grid search over: max_depth ∈ {3, 5, 7}, learning_rate ∈ {0.01, 0.1}, subsample ∈ {0.8, 1.0}. Tuning metric: validation MSE. This baseline is appropriate because: (1) it uses the same feature set as the CNN (minus imaging), isolating the effect of CNN architecture and imaging; (2) gradient boosting is a standard, well-calibrated regression approach in healthcare; (3) both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search on training/validation data). The hypothesis tests CNN + imaging vs. boosted regression without imaging, not CNN vs. poorly-tuned alternatives.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Root mean squared error (RMSE) on the held-out test set, reported in days. RMSE is the primary metric because: (1) it is in the units of the outcome (days), directly interpretable for clinical scheduling; (2) recovery time prediction is continuous with no strict threshold—RMSE penalizes both under- and over-prediction equally, aligning with the operational goal of reducing uncertainty; (3) it is symmetric and directly optimizable. Secondary reporting: mean absolute error (MAE, in days, for robustness to outliers) and coefficient of determination (R²) for variance explained. A clinically meaningful threshold is pre-specified: a difference of ≥ 1.5 days RMSE between models suggests the CNN adds value; smaller differences may not justify added complexity.
secondary_metrics: Mean absolute error (MAE) in days — robustness to outliers and interpretability for care planners
Coefficient of determination (R²) — proportion of variance explained
Mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) — percentage error, useful if hospitals think in relative terms
Calibration curve (predicted vs. actual recovery time binned by deciles) — check if predictions are reliable across the range
Performance stratified by care protocol version — confirms confound control
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning for both CNN and XGBoost is performed on the validation set (months 15–18). For CNN: early stopping is based on validation MSE; final model checkpoint is selected at the epoch with lowest validation MSE. For XGBoost: grid search is evaluated on the validation set (not cross-validation on validation data). The test set (months 19–24) is held untouched until the final evaluation step and used only once to compute RMSE, MAE, R², etc.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is created at the start of the experiment, sealed, and not accessed until model selection is complete. No hyperparameter tuning, threshold selection, or feature engineering decisions occur on the test set. After both models are finalized on the training and validation sets, the test set is used exactly once to compute the primary metric (RMSE) and secondary metrics. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap on test set residuals, 1000 iterations).
structured_choices.confound_controls: Post-operative care protocol version is a known confounder: different protocols (e.g., accelerated vs. standard recovery programs) can shift recovery times independent of model quality. Control strategy: (1) stratification by care protocol in the train/val/test split ensures each window has proportional representation of each protocol; (2) post-hoc analysis: report RMSE separately for each care protocol version within the test set to verify that the CNN's advantage (if any) holds across protocols; (3) optional: fit separate CNN and XGBoost models per protocol as a sensitivity check—if the ranking of CNN vs. XGBoost is consistent across protocols, confounding by care protocol is unlikely. Secondary confound: surgeon skill and experience (captured partially by surgeon ID). Control: surgeon ID is included as a feature in both models, and performance is stratified by surgeon ID in secondary analysis. Unmeasured confounds (e.g., patient motivation, adherence to physical therapy post-discharge) are acknowledged as limits; temporal stratification and protocol stratification reduce (but do not eliminate) their impact. The experiment establishes: CNN + imaging predicts better than boosted regression without imaging, under the assumption that care protocols and surgeon effects are balanced between train and test. It does not establish causality (e.g., why imaging helps) or generalizability to hospitals with different protocols or patient populations.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN for Knee Replacement Recovery Time Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

Data is sourced from the hospital's electronic health records, spanning a 24-month window to capture 400–600 patients with complete pre-operative MRI scans and documented post-operative recovery outcomes. The outcome variable, recovery time, is defined as the number of days from surgery to the first clinical milestone: either full weight-bearing clearance (confirmed by physical therapist) or hospital discharge, whichever occurs later. This definition is standardized by a blinded orthopedic clinician to resolve ambiguities. All records with missing MRI scans, incomplete follow-up, or conflicting outcome dates are excluded.

Features collected include: (a) demographics (age, sex, BMI); (b) pre-operative clinical history (comorbidity count, osteoarthritis severity grade, prior procedures); (c) intraoperative details (operative time, implant type, surgical approach, surgeon ID); and (d) post-operative care protocol version (enumerated by hospital protocol update dates, to control for care pathway confounds). MRI scans are acquired using the hospital's standardized knee imaging protocol and will be resampled to uniform resolution (256×256 pixels) if acquisition parameters vary.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

A stratified temporal split is used to prevent information leakage and reflect real-world deployment. The 24-month dataset is divided chronologically: months 1–14 (training, n ≈ 280 patients), months 15–18 (validation, n ≈ 90 patients), and months 19–24 (test, n ≈ 90 patients). This temporal ordering ensures that the validation and test sets represent prospective prediction tasks—as would occur in clinical deployment.

Within each temporal window, stratification is applied on two dimensions: (1) post-operative care protocol version, ensuring train, validation, and test sets have the same proportion of each protocol; (2) tertiles of age and BMI, ensuring demographic balance across splits. This dual stratification controls for known confounds (care protocol variation) and ensures fair comparison between the CNN and baseline models.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. For demographic and clinical features: numeric variables (age, BMI, operative time) are standardized using training set means and standard deviations; categorical variables (surgeon ID, surgical approach) are one-hot encoded using the training set vocabulary; missing values in clinical history are imputed with training set median/mode. For MRI images: pixel values are normalized using the training set image mean and standard deviation, and all images are resampled to 256×256 resolution.

Both the CNN and baseline model receive the same demographic/clinical/surgical feature set, ensuring a fair comparison that isolates the contribution of imaging data. The CNN receives raw MRI scans as input; the baseline (XGBoost) does not.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

**CNN Model:** A ResNet-18 backbone (pretrained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on the training set) extracts features from MRI scans. Feature maps are flattened and concatenated with demographic, clinical, and surgical features. A regression head (2–3 fully connected layers: 512 → 128 → 1) produces a continuous recovery time prediction. Loss is mean squared error (MSE); optimization uses Adam (learning rate 1e-4) with early stopping on validation MSE (patience 10 epochs). Batch size is 32.

**Baseline Model:** A gradient boosting model (XGBoost) trained on the same demographic, clinical, and surgical features *without* MRI images. Hyperparameters (max_depth, learning_rate, subsample) are tuned via grid search on the validation set, using MSE as the selection metric. This baseline is appropriate because it represents the current clinical standard (regression on structured features) and isolates the effect of imaging by design.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning for both models occurs on the validation set (months 15–18). For the CNN, early stopping is triggered when validation MSE stops improving. For XGBoost, the grid search is evaluated on the validation set, and the best-performing configuration is selected. The test set remains sealed and is used only once, at the end, for final evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Root mean squared error (RMSE) on the test set, reported in days. RMSE is chosen because it directly quantifies prediction error in clinically interpretable units, penalizes both under- and over-prediction equally, and aligns with the hospital's goal of reducing scheduling uncertainty. A pre-specified threshold of ≥1.5 days RMSE difference indicates meaningful clinical value.

**Secondary Metrics:** Mean absolute error (MAE, robustness to outliers), coefficient of determination (R², variance explained), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE, relative terms), and calibration curves (decile-binned predicted vs. actual recovery time, to assess prediction reliability).

### 7. Confound Control and Stratified Analysis

Post-operative care protocol variation is a known confounder. Control is achieved through stratified splitting (as described above) and post-hoc stratified analysis: RMSE is reported separately for each care protocol version within the test set. If the CNN's advantage is consistent across protocols, protocol-based confounding is ruled out. Surgeon ID is included as a feature in both models and is analyzed separately; performance differences across surgeons are documented to ensure no systematic bias.

This design establishes whether a CNN trained on pre-operative MRI scans predicts recovery time more accurately than gradient boosting on structured features alone, under assumptions of balanced care protocols and surgeon effects. It does not establish why imaging helps or whether findings generalize to other hospitals with different protocols or patient populations.
